## Why the trade-off matters

Predictive modelling fails in two opposite ways. A model can be too rigid and miss
real structure in the data, or it can be too flexible and chase accidental noise in
the training sample.

Bias is the systematic error that remains when the model class is too simple for the
signal we are trying to learn. Variance is the instability that appears when small
changes in the sample produce large changes in the fitted model.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students to sketch how training error and validation error move as model
flexibility increases, then use that sketch to motivate why cross-validation matters.
:::

## What students should look for

Training error alone is not a model-selection rule. As flexibility increases, training
error usually falls even when the model is becoming less useful for new data.

The operational question is therefore not “Which model fits this sample best?” but
“Which level of complexity gives the most reliable out-of-sample performance?”

## Cross-validation as the practical answer

Cross-validation is the main workflow for answering that question in `TEM 0052`.

1. Define a candidate set of models or tuning values.
2. Estimate out-of-sample performance repeatedly on held-out folds.
3. Prefer the simplest specification that performs well and remains stable across
   resamples.

This is why model assessment, hyper-parameter tuning, and the bias-variance trade-off
belong in the same lecture block: they are different views of the same generalization
problem.
